sentences.text: OK.
I forgot what we were talking about.
Oh yes.
My father's friends, they kept saying to my father, Izzy, you're stupid, you're an idiot, you're crazy.
You could be making all this money.
You're playing with your fiddle and you're fooling around with the music.
You could be making a lot of money selling the cigarettes, and the liquor, and the stockings on the black market like all of us are doing.
So one of his friends talked him into it and I always remember this.

One closet in our apartment was emptied out and it was filled with Camels, and Chesterfields, and what were those other cigar-- I forgot already what other brands they had in those days-- stacked up high to the ceiling.
Lucky Strike.
Lucky Strike, that's it.
And he would get on his bike, and he would make his rounds and his deliveries.
And he would come home absolutely sweaty and scared.
Because once you-- you know, the police, you turned in, you forget it.
After about three weeks, he said, I'm sorry, I can't do this.
I'm not made up for this.
I-- I know the money is good, but I can't help it.
I have to go back to my music.
And the Chesterfields left the apartment.
They were gone.
And we then decided, my parents-- because my father, by that time-- my Uncle Bruno was killed in Auschwitz.
All of my-- my father had two sisters and their families left in Poland-- everybody was killed.
Everybody was gone on his side.
And he decided he wanted to get out of Germany the fastest way we could.
So we applied-- we were under the Polish quota.
And we applied to go to England because my aunt-- who-- my aunt and uncle had gone to England.
But unfortunately, the uncle had died of a natural death.
But the aunt sent us-- said that she would send us an affidavit.
We applied to go to Israel, and we applied to go America.
Now my aunt who was killed in-- my uncle who was killed in Auschwitz-- my aunt survived.
And if you had a number, [BACKGROUND NOISE] you got out immediately.
There was no explanation, there were no papers, no nothing.
If you came out of a concentration camp, you had priorities to leave immediately.

So my aunt left and settled in New York because there was an uncle of my father's that, although she didn't-- she didn't seek his help, but he knew-- she knew he was there and she had communicated with him a couple of times.
And my father wrote to him and asked him to sign an affidavit.
Yes, but Brigitte, isn't it true that it was hard to get into America even with a number?
You had to have an affidavit and someone to sponsor you to come to this country.
I don't know if somebody sponsored my aunt.
Maybe somebody did.
That I don't remember.
I really don't remember.
But it was not this uncle because I'm going to tell you now, that this uncle wrote us a letter and he said, Izzy, you're a musician.
If you, you know, if you were a doctor or whatever, maybe you could come.
But musicians are a dime a dozen in this country.
And he says, you wouldn't find a job here.
You better go somewhere else.
That was his nice uncle.
So we had to wait because we were under the Polish quota.
And so I remember, I went to bed every night, I prayed.
I wanted to come to this country so badly because my aunt, who by this-- First of all, I started to go to movies and I saw films like and the And I looked at this America, and I said, oh my god.
I have to live there.
I want to live there.
I want to be there.
And my aunt had sent me a package.
And she sent me a beautiful, bright yellow wool skirt-- I remember it very well-- with red suede shoes.
And I went to school the next day with that outfit and I was like the queen of the ball.
I mean, I wouldn't take that off.
And I said, this is America, I want to be in America.
And I went to bed every night praying that I would go to America.
Even though, one summer, I think it was in '46, the synagogue that we belonged to, they had a camp.
They organized a camp.
And the-- the Americans and the Joint combined.
The Americans gave us a house that was a huge villa on the Wannsee.
I don't know who it belonged to, but it was a camp for the Youth Aliyah group.
And most of the children, most of my friends there, were orphans.
Their parents had been killed in the war or died in a concentration camp, and they were all waiting to go to Israel.
And they sent a camp director and some counselors came from Israel.
And we lived that summer-- we lived as if we were to live in a kibbutz.
And that's where I learned my first Hebrew, and I learned my first prayers.
And we used to play, sing Hebrew songs every day.
And it was just a wonderful, wonderful experience.
I didn't want to go to Israel because I just-- I had had so much hardship during the war.
And things weren't so terrific over there either.
And I-- I-- it wasn't that I didn't want to go there, I just wanted to go to America.
I mean, that was my thing.
I want to be-- I want to go to America.

And we had, because my parents were not in a concentration camp, and they were only in the ghetto for a short time in the beginning-- like six, eight months-- we have to prove that-- that we were in the ghetto.
And we had to prove that, you know, that we indeed had hardships through the Holocaust and everything else.
We had to prove who we were.
Well, one of the couples that were in-- in the ghetto with my parents gave an affidavits-- affidavit and-- and signed off that they knew my parents in the ghetto, et cetera, et cetera.
So finally, in 1949, we were given permission and papers to come here.
And we were sent to Minneapolis.

And the reason we were sent to Minneapolis-- because while we were trying to come over here, somebody told my father, he said, besides your being a musician-- just like the uncle told him-- he said, you've got to put down another profession.
So my mother wrote down that she was a seamstress.
Because my grandfather, her father, was a tailor.
And she studied designing, and she modeled hands, and she would help my father-- my grandfather sew once in a while.
Now my father, when he studied music, worked in the coat factory of his brother's in Berlin, while he was studying music.
So he put down tailor as well.
So because of the relationship of the coat factory, they sent us to Minneapolis.
Because Minneapolis had the big coat-- I don't know if you remember the storm coats.
They're gabardine coats.
They were fleece-lined.
You know, the imitation fur with the fur collars and the fur cuffs.
And where both my-- my-- when we came to Minneapolis, that's where both my parents went to work.
Because by that time, my father-- his health was failing.
And he went through tremendous emotional strains, and it affected his hearing.
Something happened to his ears, and he couldn't really be a musician professionally anymore.
He played.
He still played in the synagogue, and he joined the local unprofessional symphony in Minneapolis, and he did-- he composed.
But as a profession for him, it was over.
And he couldn't do it anymore.
And I remember it took a while but we finally left.
And we came on a troop transporter, the And all of us were refugees going to America.
And I remember I sang on the ship.
My father and I entertained.
Yes.
And-- and so we started a life in Minneapolis.
You know, we-- I went back to school, I started 11th grade, and I finished high school.
And I remember, I noticed-- I-- I have a report card where my name is still B-R-I-D-G-E-T.
And when I first came to this country, everybody spelled my name like that, and I thought, gee, that's neat.
You know, I like that.
Afterwards I found out that it was really Irish.
I said, no, I don't want to be Irish.
I went back.
So I did the spelling back to my regular name.
And I assimilated very quickly.
I tell you, my first impression was very, very disappointing.
We got off-- I thought it was Ellis Island, but it wasn't, because Ellis Island wasn't in existence at the time.
We got off the ship, and we immediately went to my aunt for the day to visit with her.
She lived off Broadway in an apartment.
And that was kind of fun and exciting.
I saw the big city, you know.
But then, you know, Warsaw and Berlin were big cities too.
Except for the skyscrapers, I had never seen skyscrapers.
And we spent the day there, and then we got back on a train that would take us from New York to Minneapolis.
And if you know that the train tracks are not in the most beautiful parts of the cities-- and we would drive, except for the country, we would drive through the cities.
And I thought, oh my god, what is this?
Where's Glenn Miller?
Where's Sonja Henie?
Where are all those beautiful places?
We had a stopover in Chicago, and we were able to get off the train for about maybe a few hours.
Because they told us that we could go out and walk the streets.
And I couldn't believe it.
I said, oh my god, this is not nice.
I mean, where are we going?
But then we got to Minneapolis, and Minneapolis is a beautiful city.
And the most incredible thing is that that's why I've worked so hard all these years.
I've-- I've made a big pledge that whatever I do or wherever I go, if it's at all possible, that I will give myself to the Federation.
That is my first priority, whatever extra money or physical health I can give.
Because they are the people who brought us here.
The Jewish Federation.
And they are the people who gave us a new life and helped us get settled here.
Because when we came to Minneapolis, there was an apartment for us.
There were furniture in the apartment.
There was a check for us to live on until my parents found the job that they needed.
And that is something that I will never, ever forget.
And so to this day, I always do what I can for others because of what has been done for me.
That I was lucky enough to survive the war, I was lucky enough to spare-- I mean, I had horrible experiences.
But they certainly don't compare to the people who were in the camps, and lost their families, and saw them killed and die in front of them.
So I feel very lucky and very fortunate in that way.
And we made a good life.
My parents-- I finished high school, and I went to the University of Minnesota, and I studied voice and drama.
And we had a wonderful life.
I became very active in the synagogue in Minneapolis, the Yediot Yeshurun, and I sang in the choir.
As a matter of fact, Rabbi Rabinowitz, who became a very famous rabbi in Washington, married Max and I.
No, I met Max here in Los Angeles.
Very interesting story.
My-- one of my very close girlfriends came to Los Angeles and married a young man from Los Angeles, and they invited me to spend a vacation with them.
And this was in 1955, I think, in November.
Now, you know what the weather's like in November in Minneapolis?
I get on a plane.
I get off the plane in Los Angeles, and there is sunshine.
There are palm trees.
There are swimming pools.
My girlfriend took me to the beauty parlor.
She made up my face.
I started to wear silver lip-- I mean, silver nail polish.
That was the in thing.
And I thought I had been dropped in paradise.
I had never seen a place like Los Angeles, and I had the most wonderful time.
And after two weeks, I get on the plane.
And I cried the whole way home.
I cried.
I get off the airplane, and it's a blinding blizzard.
The snow is going, and it's freezing.
It's 20 below zero.
And my parents are standing at the air-- in those days, you had to get off the airplane outside and walk into the building.
And they're standing there.
I didn't say hello.
I said, I'm not staying.
Just like that.
After that I said, hello, how are you?
But the first words out of my mouth were, I'm not staying.
I had sort of fallen in love with some guy that I was introduced to here, and I thought I had a mad love affair going.
Anyway, it took me about a couple of months, a few months, and I came back here.
And I settled here.
And I was going to go back and study voice and drama.
And I started working.
To make a living, I started working at an allergist's office, Dr.
Pinasse. ?]
No.
Because I had only had two years in Minneapolis.
And I was going to hopefully continue here.
And I started working at the doctor's office and my girlfriend-- I-- you know, I started going out.
I was having dates.
And-- and I had this big romance with this fellow that I had met on my first time here.
And I thought we were going to be married, but you know how these things go.
They fall through.
And I was heartbroken and my girlfriend says, you know, there's a very nice guy in my sister-in-law's building.
I think maybe we should fix you up with him.
So Max and I went on a blind date.
And we went out a couple of times, and I liked him a lot.
But after a while-- I remember, it was Passover.
And my girlfriend's sister invited me for Passover and she says, well, don't you want to invite Max?
I said, no.
I said, I don't think so, because he's really quite a bit older than I am.
Because Max is 15 years older than I am.
I said, you know, I think he's a little old for me, although I like him a lot.
So I finished the dating and about three, four weeks later, Max and I end up at Canter's on Fairfax.
I'm there with my girlfriends, and he's there alone with his newspaper.
And he comes and sits, and he always tells the story that I squeezed his hand, but I know he squeezed mine.
And that started the romance all over again.
And we were-- in three months, we got engaged, which today, is unheard of.
You know, you have to have long relationship.
And three months after that, we were married.
How was I different?
I-- I think I am very, very fortunate because I know that people-- the Holocaust affected people-- I mean, this is my impression.
The Holocaust affected people two ways.
You either were totally emotionally scarred or the experiences gave you strength and made a very strong person out of you.
And I think I'm very lucky that I became that strong person.
Because the experiences made me an adult very quickly.
And it made-- it gave me strength to go through a lot of other things.
And maybe that's why I fell in love with my husband.
I couldn't communicate with-- with the giddy guys that were my age.
They didn't have the substance for me.
And I think that is the only good thing, I think, that came out of it for me.
That it gave me a lot of strength to-- to be the person that I hope I am.
[SIGHS] Well, I had a difficult time with my father.
Because of his-- I don't know if it had to do with his scars of the war or whatever.
My father had a very strange personality.
Even though I loved him very much, it was-- it was hard.
He was very strict, and he still had the very old world attitudes.
And here we are in Minneapolis.
You know, it's now-- [LAUGHS] I'm 15 years old.
Or no, 16.
Excuse me, I'm 16.
So by that time I was 17 years old.
I would always have to be home at a certain time.
If I wasn't home, he would stand at the top of the stairs and really be furious with me.
And my father had a very, very bad way.
Although at the time, I couldn't correct it.
He would never be violent or hit me or anything.
He would become silent.
He would become angry.
And when my mother sided with me or he was angry at both of us, it could be sometimes days before he spoke to us.
So there was this constant silence in the apartment.
And my mother, I felt bad for my mother.
Because my mother, you have to remember, was ill.
My mother still had to fight her diabetes.
She had to adjust again to a different diet.
You know, now we're eating again, and the insulin is there.
And-- and you have to adjust your system.
And she would go into insulin shock many, many times, and it would create a strain.
But we clung-- we hung together, we did.
Not at first.
They moved here when I became pregnant with my first child.
I have three children.
A 35-year-old daughter, a 33-year-old daughter, and a 29-year-old son.
I hope so.
Yes.
I-- I was very fortunate in-- in that I-- it's very interesting.
I'm either fortunate and then lately, I've been thinking that maybe I wasn't so fortunate.
Because at the time that we married, the style was you married.
You became a wife to your husband.
You had children, if your husband was comfortable enough where you didn't have to work.
But you didn't even-- you didn't pursue a career.
So I dropped all my talents completely and became the mother, and-- which was fine.
I mean, to me, that was the new thing and that was the right thing to do.
And Max and I, we joined organizations.
I joined Hadassah.
We joined the Federation.
I became very active in diabetic causes because of my mother.
And so I became very, very involved in the community.
But I neglected my talents, and I only used them for charity organizations, for private parties.
I didn't pursue my career.
And today, I'm kind of sorry because if I would have been young today, even if I'm married, I mean, I would pursue my career.
My daughters both have full careers.
My son was just married and his wife, they all have careers.
And they pursue their dreams, even though they're married.
But I can't complain.
I have a wonderful family.
I have had a tremendous married life.
My husband and I have been very fortunate.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We're married 38 years, which is, in today's times, I guess, unusual.
And we're still in love, and we're still happy with each other.
And our children have all become responsible adults, and I'm very proud of them.
So I guess in a way, I've done OK.
I think I would say to them that I was glad that I was able to make this tape.
And that I'm glad that I'm living in a time where we can pass on heritage and experiences that we've had to our children, and great children, and great-great-grandchildren.
That they will see how the family evolved and how we lived.
And that especially because of the war years and the Holocaust, that they can see that out of this, still people come and survive.
And hopefully that the Jewish people will always survive.
And I think that's-- that's my wish.
That we continue to be strong and that our children and our children's children hopefully will carry on where we left off.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's my legacy.
I don't know.
I mean, he-- first of all, his background is just as strong rooted as mine.
He-- his father was killed in the pogroms.
He was born in Russia--
--and his mother and his family came out of the pogroms and went to Canada.
And that's where his family immigrated to.
No, but Max will know.
I don't remember.
He was a little baby.
Yes.
OK.
My daughter, my oldest daughter, 35, is married and has been married for 11 years to a wonderful young man.
And they are both very intelligent, and very productive people, and I'm very, very proud of them.
My Cynthia, my 33-year-old, is also a career woman, and very successful, and just got engaged, and is going to be married next summer.
And my baby, my son, who is 29, was just married, god, a week and a half ago.
Almost-- it'll be two weeks on Saturday.
They are all going to be here today.
Yes.
